messages.say:
Thought for 6s


Other stable ID types already implied by the system (even if you’ve only implemented Document IDs end-to-end so far) fall into a few buckets: artifacts, governance, execution, observability, and quality/conflict tracking.


Artifact / Knowledge IDs (things your system “knows about”)


template_id — identifies a reusable template definition (e.g., TMPL-...). Used by SUB_TEMPLATES and referenced by pattern-to-template guidance.
23_TEMPLATE_HIERARCHY_AND_SELEC…
12_DATA_SCHEMA_INVENTORY

pattern_id — identifies a pattern in the pattern catalog (e.g., PAT-...). Used by SUB_PATTERNS; can be referenced by templates.
12_DATA_SCHEMA_INVENTORY
11_INTER_SUBSYSTEM_CONTRACT_CAT…

schema_id — identifies a schema in the central schema inventory (e.g., SCH-...). Used to prevent schema drift/duplication and to declare authority/ownership.
12_DATA_SCHEMA_INVENTORY

path_key — stable identifier for a canonical path entry in SUB_PATH_REGISTRY (not a raw filesystem path).
12_DATA_SCHEMA_INVENTORY
11_INTER_SUBSYSTEM_CONTRACT_CAT…

glossary term key — the term itself is effectively the ID (canonical term string). Governed by SUB_GLOSSARY.
14_GLOSSARY_AUTHORITY_AND_SCOPE


Governance / Policy IDs (things that “rule” the system)


rule_id — identifies SSOT validation rules (e.g., RULE-...).
11_INTER_SUBSYSTEM_CONTRACT_CAT…
12_DATA_SCHEMA_INVENTORY

policy_id — versioned governance policies (format shown as POLICY-{DOMAIN}-{NUMBER}.v{VERSION}).
06_SSOT_GOVERNANCE_MODEL

conflict_id — identifies a known conflict in the conflict register (e.g., CONFLICT #001).
05_KNOWN_CONFLICTS_REGISTER

precedent_id — identifies an authority ruling precedent (e.g., PREC-...).
08_SSOT_TO_SUBSYSTEM_AUTHORITY_…


Execution IDs (things that “run”)


contract_id — identifies pipeline I/O contracts (e.g., CNT-...).
12_DATA_SCHEMA_INVENTORY

directory_id — identifies a directory contract/coverage target (a directory as a governed unit).
23_TEMPLATE_HIERARCHY_AND_SELEC…
12_DATA_SCHEMA_INVENTORY

pipeline_id — identifies an executing pipeline instance (state persistence uses pipeline_{id}.json).
19_PIPELINE_EXECUTION_MODEL

evaluation_id — identifies a directory evaluation run (e.g., EVAL-...).
13_API_SURFACE_MAP


Relationship / Graph IDs (things that “connect”)


relationship_id — identifies schema-to-schema relationship edges (e.g., REL-001).
12_DATA_SCHEMA_INVENTORY

document_graph node IDs — in your cross-reference index, the node key is the document_id, and edges are typed (REFERENCES, DERIVED_FROM, etc.).
17_CROSS_REFERENCE_INDEX


Observability / Error IDs (things you debug and audit)


error_code — standard error taxonomy ID (e.g., ERR-{SUBSYSTEM}-{NUMBER}).
11_INTER_SUBSYSTEM_CONTRACT_CAT…
13_API_SURFACE_MAP

audit event identity — not explicitly named as an event_id, but the audit log schema is a cross-system contract; if you want stable event correlation, an event_id is the missing piece.
06_SSOT_GOVERNANCE_MODEL
12_DATA_SCHEMA_INVENTORY


QA / Drift IDs (things that flag system health)


drift_id — identifies a schema drift finding (e.g., DRIFT-001).
12_DATA_SCHEMA_INVENTORY

duplication_id — identifies suspected schema duplication findings (e.g., DUP-001).
12_DATA_SCHEMA_INVENTORY



“Trigger ID” specifically


A trigger_id is not explicitly defined in the files you shared (no schema/pattern shown for it), but your architecture clearly uses triggers (CI hooks, event-driven orchestration, pipeline triggers).


01_SYSTEM_ARCHITECTURE_MAP


19_PIPELINE_EXECUTION_MODEL


06_SSOT_GOVERNANCE_MODEL


If you add it, it should be a first-class stable ID for:


CI hook invocations

file-drop/watch events

scheduled jobs

orchestration chain starts


Typical companions you’ll want alongside trigger_id:


run_id / execution_id (correlates everything spawned by a trigger)

correlation_id (cross-subsystem trace)

orchestration_chain_id (which automation chain definition fired)


Those aren’t formally in the current artifacts, but they fit cleanly into your existing “SSOT orchestrates, subsystems execute, audit logs unify” model.


06_SSOT_GOVERNANCE_MODEL


08_SSOT_TO_SUBSYSTEM_AUTHORITY_…

Yes—if you want AI-safe cross-referencing, you eventually need stable IDs below the file level (module/class/function/method/etc.). The practical way to do it is: don’t try to make the name itself the ID. Names change. Use an ID plus a resolver.


Stable ID types for Python code elements


1) symbol_id (recommended umbrella)


A stable ID for any code element (“symbol”):


module

class

function

method

property

constant/variable (optional)

import alias (optional)


Example shape:


SYM-PY-FUNC-01J... (ULID)

SYM-PY-CLASS-01J...


This is what everything else should reference.


2) definition_id (aka “decl_id”)


If you need “this exact definition site” (not just the conceptual function), use a distinct ID:


survives formatting changes

changes if the definition is deleted/recreated elsewhere (depending on your rules)


Often you can collapse this into symbol_id unless you need refactoring analytics.


3) callsite_id (optional)


Stable identifier for a callsite location if you’re doing deep ref graphs (“this call in this file at this AST node”).


Useful for static analysis and “what changed” diffs, but not required for most doc-id systems.



How to make function IDs stable in real codebases


You need two layers:


A) A “best-effort identity key” (derived, not authoritative)


Compute a key from facts that tend to remain stable:


path_key (from your path registry; not raw path)

qualified_name (module + class + function)

kind (function/method/etc.)

signature_fingerprint (args, kwonly, defaults shape, annotations optionally)

semantic_fingerprint (AST-based hash that ignores whitespace/comments; optionally ignores variable renames)


This key is used to match a symbol across scans.


B) An authoritative registry (authoritative)


Maintain symbol_registry.jsonl (or SQLite) that maps:


symbol_id → current path_key, current qualified_name, fingerprints, first_seen, last_seen, status


When the function moves/renames, you resolve it by matching fingerprints + ancestry context and keep the same symbol_id.



Where to store the ID


You have three workable options:


Option 1 (best for Python): sidecar registry only


No code edits required. IDs live in registry, discovered via AST scan + matching.


Pros: zero intrusion, no comment drift, no style fights

Cons: harder to review in PRs without tooling


Option 2: inline comment tag on the def line


Example:


def foo(x: int) -> int:  # sym_id: SYM-PY-FUNC-01J...
    ...


Pros: easy grepping, local visibility

Cons: linters/formatters, manual editing risk


Option 3: docstring header tag


Example:


def foo(x: int) -> int:
    """sym_id: SYM-PY-FUNC-01J..."""


Pros: survives formatting, less inline noise

Cons: pollutes docs; people forget to keep it


For your “deterministic, zero-touch” goal: Option 1 + automated resolver is the cleanest.



Extraction tooling (reliable for .py)


ast (stdlib): solid for structure; limited for round-trip + exact formatting.

libcst: concrete syntax tree; best for stable edits and comment/docstring insertion.

tree-sitter-python: great for fast structural indexing across many files.


If you want stable fingerprints for identity matching, use AST normalized hashing (tree-sitter or ast), and if you want automated ID insertion, use libcst.



Minimal “done” spec for function-level IDs


Implement these artifacts:


symbol_registry (JSONL or SQLite)
symbol_id


doc_id (file doc_id)

path_key

kind

qualified_name

signature_fp

body_fp

first_seen, last_seen

status (active, moved, deleted)

supersedes[] / superseded_by (optional)
symbol_scan command


scans .py

updates registry deterministically

emits symbol_manifest.json for the run
symbol_resolver rule set


match precedence: explicit tag > strong fingerprint match > weak match > new symbol_id


That gives you stable IDs for functions even through renames and moves—without betting the system on brittle names or line numbers.

Thought for 10s


Other stable ID types (beyond doc_id)


Your repo already implies (or explicitly names) multiple stable-ID families that are not “file identity,” even if only doc_id is fully implemented today.


A) Repository identity (non-file)


dir_id (directory identity): already part of governance conventions (.dir_id, DIR_CONTRACT.yaml, DIR_MANIFEST.yaml). It’s a stable ID for a directory as a governed unit.
DIRECTORY_INDEX

run_id (pipeline run identity): the decision record reserves ULIDs for runtime tracking like run/event IDs.
DECISION_RECORD_STRUCTURED_DOC_…

event_id (ledger/event identity): same ULID reservation for JSONL ledgers / event streams.
DECISION_RECORD_STRUCTURED_DOC_…

trace_id (observability correlation): appears as a required parameter pattern in repo guidance (ties into determinism/telemetry).
CLAUDE


B) “Definition artifacts” (things the pipeline reasons about)


pattern_id: stable ID for an execution pattern/template (you already treat “PAT” as a category in doc_id, but that’s still a file doc_id, not an ID for the abstract pattern as a first-class entity).

requirement_id: stable ID for requirements (“R-…”) mentioned in your stable-ID conventions.
CLAUDE

artifact_id: stable IDs for generated artifacts (“A-…”) mentioned in conventions.
CLAUDE

section_id: stable IDs for sections (“S-…”) mentioned in conventions.
CLAUDE

schema_id / contract_id: stable ID for schemas and I/O contracts (you already treat “SPEC” as a doc_id category; that still doesn’t give you a stable identity for the schema/contract across file splits/merges).

validator_id / gate_id: stable ID for validators and gates (since you treat validators as first-class scripts and CI gates).

trigger_id: stable ID for automation triggers (watcher rules, hooks, scheduled tasks).

prompt_id: stable ID for prompt “assets” (you already do this elsewhere in the larger system, and the governance language implies it).
CLAUDE

term_id: stable ID for glossary terms (you already have a glossary category and subsystem references; same pattern applies).



Stable IDs for .py elements (functions/classes/etc.)


This is the next logical tier: symbol identity (call it symbol_id).


There are three viable approaches:


Derived (deterministic) symbol_id (recommended default)
Build symbol_id from stable inputs:

file_doc_id (the file’s identity)

qualified_name (module.class.function)

signature_fingerprint (args/return annotations when present)

optionally a code anchor hash (hash of a normalized AST node)


Example shape:
SYM-{FILE_DOC_ID}-{QUALNAME_HASH}-{SIG_HASH}

Pros: no “minting” step, no collision risk if done right, fully reproducible.

Cons: changes if you rename/move symbol or change signature (you can mitigate with aliasing).
Minted symbol_id (like doc_id but for symbols)


A registry issues SYM-...-0001 style IDs, then you inject them.

Pros: survives renames/signature changes.

Cons: hard injection problem (where do you store it?), and harder refactor flows.
Hybrid


Use derived IDs for the majority; mint stable IDs only for public API symbols that need long-lived identity.

This matches your “structured doc_id for documents, ULID for runtime” philosophy: use stable where it matters, derived where it’s cheaper.
DECISION_RECORD_STRUCTURED_DOC_…


Where to store symbol_id


Sidecar index (recommended): symbols_inventory.jsonl + SYMBOL_REGISTRY.yaml (or JSON) keyed by file_doc_id.

Optional inline annotations only for exported/public symbols:
comment immediately above def/class:
# SYMBOL_ID: SYM-...

You already have an AST extractor in the doc_id subsystem (tree_sitter_extractor.py), which is exactly the kind of machinery you’d reuse for symbol discovery.
DIRECTORY_INDEX



How similar is this to doc_id? What’s reusable?


Your doc_id system already defines a reusable “identity subsystem pattern”:


DocID pattern (what you already implemented)


Scanner / Discovery: enumerate eligible files; extract identity marker; emit inventory snapshot (docs_inventory.jsonl).
DOC_ID_SYSTEM_COMPLETE_SPECIFIC…

Assigner / Injection: mint IDs + inject into targets (file-type-specific injection rules).

Registry (SSOT): authoritative DOC_ID_REGISTRY.yaml + category counters + entries.

Sync: reconcile inventory ↔ registry; detect drift/missing files.
DOC_ID_SYSTEM_COMPLETE_SPECIFIC…

Validators: format/uniqueness/sync/coverage/reference checks.

Automation: git hooks, watcher, scheduled tasks.

Reporting: coverage trends, alerts.

Tests + CLI wrapper: enforce determinism + regression safety.


That entire lifecycle is reusable for pattern_id / trigger_id / symbol_id / validator_id, with only two things changing:


What changes per stable-ID family


Extractor
doc_id: simple header/frontmatter parsing.


symbol_id: AST-based extraction (Tree-sitter / Python ast), plus edge discovery (calls/imports) if you want relationships.
Injection strategy


doc_id: always inject into file content.

symbol_id: often better as sidecar registry (and only selectively injected).


Everything else (inventory, registry, validators, watchers, reports, tests, CLI) is the same “subsystem skeleton.”



Concrete planning reuse: “Identity Subsystem Template” (for any stable ID)


If you want other stable IDs to feel like doc_id (zero-touch, governed, measurable), replicate the same 7 functional areas you already standardized in SUB_DOC_ID:


1_CORE_OPERATIONS/ → scan/extract + (optional) assign/inject

2_VALIDATION_FIXING/ → uniqueness, drift, coverage, reference integrity

3_AUTOMATION_HOOKS/ → watcher rules, git hooks, scheduler installers

4_REPORTING_MONITORING/ → coverage dashboards + alerts

5_REGISTRY_DATA/ → SSOT registry + inventory snapshots

6_TESTS/ → regression suite for extract/assign/validate

7_CLI_INTERFACE/ → unified CLI commands mirroring doc_id


You already documented this operating model as the canonical structure for the doc_id system.



Recommended “next stable IDs” order (leveraging maximum doc_id reuse)


trigger_id (lowest complexity, highest automation payoff)
You already have watchers/hooks/scheduled tasks; give each trigger rule a stable ID and registry.


Lets you audit “what automation exists” and enforce “no untracked trigger changes.”
pattern_id (big payoff for deterministic execution)


Treat patterns as entities with IDs independent of the file that stores them.

Enables “pattern binding” and “pattern coverage” reporting.
symbol_id (functions/classes) (highest complexity)


Start with sidecar-only inventory + registry.

Add injection only for public API symbols if/when you need long-lived IDs.


This sequencing matches your existing governance stance: structured, human-readable IDs for durable artifacts; ULIDs reserved for runtime trace/run/event identity.


DECISION_RECORD_STRUCTURED_DOC_…
